title: Array representation and operations

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Array representation and operations; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Array representation and operations
sources.claim: Arrays support constant-time indexed access while insertion or deletion away from the end usually shifts a linear suffix.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; material applicable to Array representation and operations
sources.claim: Arrays support constant-time indexed access while insertion or deletion away from the end usually shifts a linear suffix.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Arrays support constant-time indexed access while insertion or deletion away from the end usually shifts a linear suffix.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Inserting at index two of five values shifts three existing values before writing the new one. Your goal is to implement and test core array operations and explain their complexity. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Readiness, algorithms and analysis; Time, space and asymptotic growth; Selected-language readiness. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Arrays support constant-time indexed access while insertion or deletion away from the end usually shifts a linear suffix. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Dynamic arrays add capacity and amortized append, while bounds, element size, and language representation differ. The draft therefore rejects: Every array operation is constant time because indexing is constant time.

includes: Analyze indexed access, traversal, insertion, deletion, resizing and copying costs.
Implement and test core array operations and explain their complexity.

In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.
excludes: Dynamic arrays add capacity and amortized append, while bounds, element size, and language representation differ.

outcomes: Implement and test core array operations and explain their complexity.

examples.title: Array representation and operations: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Inserting at index two of five values shifts three existing values before writing the new one.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Inserting at index two of five values shifts three existing values before writing the new one.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Separate indexed access from structural updates; middle insertion and deletion require linear movement in an array.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because Separate indexed access from structural updates; middle insertion and deletion require linear movement in an array.

examples.title: Array representation and operations: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, carry pointer/array bounds and explicit storage; in C++, use span, array, string, or vector with invalidation rules; in Java, use arrays, String, and collection interfaces with checked indexes; in Python, use list and str while accounting for slicing, immutability, and object overhead.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Dynamic arrays add capacity and amortized append, while bounds, element size, and language representation differ.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every array operation is constant time because indexing is constant time.
misconceptions.correction: Separate indexed access from structural updates; middle insertion and deletion require linear movement in an array.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Every array operation is constant time because indexing is constant time.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Array representation and operations, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
Dynamic arrays add capacity and amortized append, while bounds, element size, and language representation differ.
